result:
The first thing I remember is a color. Not a shape or a face or a sound, but a color—a deep, saturated blue, like the sky just after the sun has dropped below the horizon, when the world holds its breath before night truly falls. I must have been very young, because the memory has no context, no before or after. It simply is: a vast expanse of blue, and a feeling of absolute peace. For years, I thought it was a memory of looking up at the evening sky from my childhood backyard, lying on the grass while the grown-ups talked in low voices nearby. But recently, my mother told me that when I was a toddler, my favorite blanket was that exact shade of blue. I carried it everywhere until it disintegrated. So perhaps my first memory is not a moment of cosmic awareness, but the intimate comfort of a piece of fabric pressed against my cheek. Memory is like that—a collage of fragments, a story we tell ourselves, constantly edited, rewritten, and sometimes entirely fabricated.

I’ve been thinking about memory a lot lately, partly because I’m getting older and the past seems to be accumulating at an alarming rate, and partly because I recently found a box of old photographs in my parents’ attic. The photographs were a jumble of moments: birthdays, vacations, awkward school portraits, candid shots of people whose names I’ve forgotten. As I sifted through them, I was struck by how many of the events I didn’t remember at all. There I was, age seven, grinning gap-toothed next to a sandcastle, and I have no recollection of that beach trip. There I was at a family reunion, surrounded by cousins, and the entire day is a blank. It’s a disconcerting feeling, to see proof of a moment you lived but cannot access. It’s as if that version of me is a stranger, someone who existed once and then vanished, leaving behind only a paper avatar.

Neuroscientists tell us that memory is not a fixed recording but a reconstructive process. Every time we recall something, we are essentially re-creating it, pulling together bits of sensory information, emotion, and narrative, and in the process, we subtly alter it. The act of remembering changes the memory itself. This means that our most cherished, most often-revisited memories are likely the least accurate. They have been worn smooth by handling, like river stones, their original sharp edges rounded into something more pleasing, more mythological. I think about the story my family tells about the time I got lost in a department store when I was five. I remember the terror of being surrounded by racks of clothes, the forest of adult legs, the tears streaming down my face. But I also remember a kind woman in a red vest who found me and gave me a lollipop. My mother’s version is different: she says I was found by a security guard, a gruff man who scared me even more, and there was no lollipop. Whose memory is correct? Probably neither. We have both reshaped the event to fit our emotional needs—mine for a comforting rescuer, hers perhaps for a moment of maternal guilt sharpened by a stranger’s incompetence.

This malleability of memory has always fascinated and troubled me. If our identities are built on the scaffold of our memories, what does it mean if that scaffold is constantly shifting? Are we not then a different person each time we remember who we are? The philosopher John Locke thought that personal identity is tied to memory; we are the same person over time because we can remember our past thoughts and actions. But if memory is a fiction, then the self is a fiction too, a story we tell ourselves to make sense of a chaotic stream of experience. I find this both terrifying and liberating. Terrifying because it suggests there is no solid ground beneath our feet, no unchanging core. Liberating because it means we are not bound by the past; we can, in a very real sense, rewrite our own history. We are the authors of our own narrative, and like any good author, we can revise.

This brings me to the question of forgetting. We tend to see forgetting as a failure, a flaw in the system. We mourn the memories that slip away, the names and faces that fade, the precious moments that dissolve into the fog. But forgetting is essential. Imagine if we remembered everything, every single detail of every single day. The Argentine writer Jorge Luis Borges explored this in his story “Funes the Memorious,” about a man who, after an accident, acquires perfect memory. Funes can remember every leaf on every tree he has ever seen, every moment of every day, but this superhuman ability becomes a curse. He is so overwhelmed by the sheer volume of detail that he cannot generalize, cannot think abstractly, cannot even sleep because his mind is perpetually flooded with the past. To think is to suppress difference, to categorize, to forget. Without forgetting, we would be paralyzed. Our brains are designed to sift, to prioritize, to discard. The vast majority of our experiences are consigned to oblivion, and that is a mercy.

But what determines what stays and what goes? Why do I remember the blue blanket but not the beach trip? Emotion plays a huge role. Memories that are tied to strong feelings—fear, joy, shame, wonder—are more likely to be encoded deeply. The amygdala, the brain’s emotional center, interacts with the hippocampus, the seat of memory formation, during emotionally charged events, essentially flagging them as important: “Remember this; it matters.” This is why we have so many memories from moments of crisis or ecstasy, and why the mundane days blur into a gray wash. Looking back on my own life, I realize my memories are not a continuous film but a spray of bright points, constellations of feeling. The first day of school, the first kiss, the death of a grandparent, the time I won a poetry prize, the time I crashed my bike and broke my arm—these moments are vivid, sensory-rich, while whole years of school, entire friendships, have faded to a vague outline.

But there is another curious category: the memories that seem utterly insignificant, devoid of any obvious emotional charge, and yet they persist. The other day I remembered, out of nowhere, the pattern of the linoleum floor in my elementary school cafeteria. It was a geometric design in shades of beige and brown, meant to hide dirt. I can picture it perfectly. Why has my brain held onto that piece of trivia for thirty years? There is no obvious reason. Perhaps it was the backdrop for some small, forgotten drama—a moment of embarrassment or connection—and the floor was encoded alongside the emotion. Or perhaps memory is simply more chaotic than we like to think, subject to random firings of neurons, a lottery of retention. These stray memories feel like postcards from a past self, messages without context. “Here is a floor,” my past self says. “I thought you might want to know.” And I do, strangely. I do.

I have a theory that we don’t just remember events; we remember the feeling of being a certain age. There is a particular quality of light, a texture of experience, that belongs to each era of our lives. Childhood has a kind of eternal summer glow, a sense of time stretching out infinitely, each day a vast continent to explore. Adolescence is all sharp edges and shadows, intense and melodramatic, every emotion cranked to maximum volume. Early adulthood is a whirl of possibility and anxiety, the future a foggy terrain you’re trying to navigate with a faulty map. And now, in middle age, time feels different again—faster, more precious, more weighted with the awareness of its finitude. When I remember a specific moment from, say, high school, I’m not just remembering the event; I’m re-inhabiting the skin of my teenage self, feeling the particular blend of insecurity and longing that defined those years. It’s a form of time travel, however imperfect.

Music is one of the most powerful triggers for this kind of time travel. A certain song can instantly transport me to a specific place and time, with a vividness that is almost hallucinatory. The smell of rain on hot pavement, the taste of cheap beer, the face of someone I haven’t thought about in decades—all can come rushing back on a few bars of melody. Psychologists call this “music-evoked autobiographical memory,” and it seems to have a unique potency, perhaps because music engages so many brain regions simultaneously, including those involved in emotion, motor planning, and memory. The soundtrack of our lives is a library of selves. There are albums I cannot listen to because they are too saturated with a past relationship, and hearing them would be like opening a door I prefer to keep closed. But there are other songs that are like old friends, comfortable and reassuring, reminders of who I once was and how far I’ve come.

I’ve been trying, recently, to be more intentional about memory. In a world where we document everything with our phones, snapping photos and videos of every meal, every sunset, every concert, I wonder if we are actually outsourcing our memory to devices. Studies suggest that when we take a photo of something, we remember it less clearly, because our brain knows the information is stored externally—a phenomenon known as the “Google effect.” We are creating vast archives of our lives, but are we truly experiencing them? There’s a difference between capturing a moment and inhabiting it. I’ve started keeping a handwritten journal, not a detailed log of daily events, but a collection of small observations, fragments of conversation, dreams, snippets of sensory data. The act of writing by hand, slow and deliberate, forces me to pay attention, to sift through the day’s chaos and find the kernel of meaning. It’s a way of telling my brain: “This matters. Hold onto this.” I don’t know if it will work. Maybe in thirty years I will remember the pattern of the linoleum floor and not the carefully recorded wisdom. But the practice itself feels valuable, a small rebellion against the erosion of time.

Time, of course, is the medium of memory. We talk about time as if it were a river, flowing in one direction, carrying us along. But memory disrupts that linear flow. In memory, time is a hall of mirrors, a Mobius strip, a tangled ball of string. The past is not fixed; it is constantly being reshaped by the present. And the future, in turn, is shaped by our remembered past. The writer Rebecca Solnit has a beautiful image: “The past is not a place you can travel back to, but a landscape that travels with you.” I carry my childhood with me, not as a set of data points, but as a set of lenses through which I see the world. My fear of abandonment is, no doubt, rooted in some early, half-remembered loss. My love of silence comes from growing up in a noisy house. My taste in art, my political beliefs, my sense of humor—all are products of a history I can only partially recall. We are made of memory, even the memories we’ve forgotten.

There is a particular kind of melancholy that comes with watching your parents age. Seeing them begin to forget—names, dates, the thread of a story—is a preview of our own future. My father, who once had an encyclopedic memory for historical facts, now sometimes struggles to recall what he had for breakfast. Dementia is a cruel thief, taking not just the ability to form new memories but the old ones too, stripping away the layers of self until the person you knew is replaced by a stranger. It is a reminder that memory is a biological process, dependent on the delicate machinery of the brain, and that machinery can fail. I try not to think about it too much, but it’s there, a shadow at the edge of every family gathering. I find myself paying closer attention now, trying to memorize the cadence of my mother’s laughter, the way my father’s eyes crinkle when he smiles. I want to hold onto these things, even as I know that memory is unreliable, that one day I will misplace these moments just as I misplaced that beach trip. Perhaps writing them down is part of that attempt to build an ark against the flood.

But ultimately, I think the imperfection of memory is its beauty. A perfect memory would be a repository of dead facts, a library without a ghost. What makes a memory alive is the way it changes, the way it acquires layers of meaning over time. The blue blanket, the lost lollipop, the cafeteria floor—these are not just data; they are part of the ongoing project of making sense of my existence. They are the raw material of story, and story is how we understand everything. We are narrative creatures, and memory is our first and most important editor, selecting, arranging, embellishing, omitting. It gives us a coherent identity, a sense of trajectory. Even when it deceives us, it is trying to tell the truth—the emotional truth, if not the factual one.

So I embrace the lies my memory tells me. I embrace the invented conversations, the conflated events, the details borrowed from dreams or photographs. They are my mythology, the sacred texts of my selfhood. When I recall that moment of standing in a sunlit kitchen with a former lover, the light falling just so, the smell of coffee in the air, I don’t care if it happened exactly that way. It feels true. It captures something about the relationship that a forensic account could not. The memory is a poem, not a police report. And as I get older, I find I am more interested in poetry than in reports.

Lately, I have been trying an experiment: I take a memory, one that I have rehearsed many times, and I try to remember it differently. I shift the perspective, change the lighting, imagine a different ending. It feels transgressive, like I am tampering with evidence. But it also feels powerful. If memory is an act of imagination, then why not imagine boldly? Why not rewrite the painful memory to be slightly less painful, to give myself the compassion I couldn’t give at the time? This is not about denying reality; it’s about recognizing that the memory is already a reconstruction, and I have some agency in how it’s built. There is a therapeutic technique called “rescripting,” used for trauma, where patients revisit a traumatic memory and imagine a different, safer outcome. The brain, it turns out, can be tricked into reducing the emotional charge of a memory by altering the narrative. It’s a kind of lucid dreaming into the past.

I tried this with a memory of a childhood humiliation, a moment on the playground when an older kid pushed me down and everyone laughed. For years, that memory had a sharp, hot quality; it could still make me flinch. I decided to re-imagine it: I saw myself standing up, brushing off my knees, and walking away with dignity. I saw the other kids not laughing but looking at me with respect. I know that’s not what happened. But after I did this exercise, the memory’s power over me diminished. It became just a story, one of many, and I could choose to tell it differently. It felt like I had reclaimed something.

This fluidity extends beyond individual memory to collective memory—the stories we tell as families, communities, nations. History is not what happened; history is the story we agree to tell about what happened. And that agreement is always contested, always political, always shifting. Monuments are built and torn down, textbooks are rewritten, archives are opened and sealed. The past is a battleground because whoever controls the memory controls the identity of the group. We are seeing this play out in real time across the world, as societies grapple with the legacies of colonialism, slavery, war. The fight is not just about what to do in the present; it’s about how to remember the past. And the stakes are existential. If you can change the memory, you can change the identity.

I think about my own family’s collective memory, the stories that get told at every holiday gathering. There’s the story of how my grandparents met, the story of my uncle’s cross-country road trip, the story of the time the turkey caught fire. These stories are polished from decades of retelling, and they have taken on a mythic quality. They define us. When my grandmother died, we lost not just her presence but her version of these stories. Now my mother is the keeper, and one day I will be. I am already noticing how my versions differ from hers, how I emphasize certain details and forget others. The inheritance is a living thing, and it will evolve in my hands. That’s a responsibility I don’t take lightly. I want to honor the past while also allowing it to breathe.

And what of the future memories? The brain region crucial for remembering the past—the hippocampus—is also vital for imagining the future. People with damage to the hippocampus cannot imagine novel scenes; they are stuck in a perpetual present. This suggests that memory and imagination are two sides of the same coin. We use the building blocks of remembered experience to construct possible futures. In a very real sense, we remember forward. So the memories I am making now are not just a record of where I’ve been; they are the palette with which I will paint my tomorrows. This makes me want to live more richly, to gather vivid, varied experiences that can serve as material for future dreams. Travel, art, conversation, risk—these are investments in a memory bank I will draw on for the rest of my life.

I recently went back to my childhood home, the one with the backyard where I might have seen that blue sky. The house seemed smaller, of course, the yard less vast. The tree I used to climb is gone, cut down years ago. The swing set is a patch of rust. Standing there, I felt a surge of ghosts—the echoes of a thousand summer afternoons, the faint laughter of the child I used to be. It was a haunting, but a gentle one. I realized that place is not a museum of fixed memories; it’s a source of raw material, and I have been using it all my life to build a self. That self is not a finished product. It is a story under constant revision, a river that changes its course with every bend. And the blue that started it all—whether sky or blanket—is still there, a deep current running beneath everything, the color of the beginning. I can’t grasp it, not really, but I can feel it, a presence as close as my own breath, a reminder that we are, each of us, made of the past, and yet we are free to become something new.

And what of the future memories? The brain region crucial for remembering the past—the hippocampus—is also vital for imagining the future. People with damage to the hippocampus cannot imagine novel scenes; they are stuck in a perpetual present. This suggests that memory and imagination are two sides of the same coin. We use the building blocks of remembered experience to construct possible futures. In a very real sense, we remember forward. So the memories I am making now are not just a record of where I’ve been; they are the palette with which I will paint my tomorrows. This makes me want to live more richly, to gather vivid, varied experiences that can serve as material for future dreams. Travel, art, conversation, risk—these are investments in a memory bank I will draw on for the rest of my life.

I recently went back to my childhood home, the one with the backyard where I might have seen that blue sky. The house seemed smaller, of course, the yard less vast. The tree I used to climb is gone, cut down years ago. The swing set is a patch of rust. Standing there, I felt a surge of ghosts—the echoes of a thousand summer afternoons, the faint laughter of the child I used to be. It was a haunting, but a gentle one. I realized that place is not a museum of fixed memories; it’s a source of raw material, and I have been using it all my life to build a self. That self is not a finished product. It is a story under constant revision, a river that changes its course with every bend. And the blue that started it all—whether sky or blanket—is still there, a deep current running beneath everything, the color of the beginning. I can’t grasp it, not really, but I can feel it, a presence as close as my own breath, a reminder that we are, each of us, made of the past, and yet we are free to become something new.
